_comment: Generic, non-personalised education: what each biomarker IS and its NORMAL physiological function only. Must NEVER describe what abnormal levels indicate or cause (the Step 6 education line in BIOMARKER_INTENDED_PURPOSE.md). Keyed by source_biomarker_name. Biomarkers without an entry simply have no education available.

entries.name: LDL Cholesterol
entries.what_it_is: LDL (low-density lipoprotein) is one of the particles that carries cholesterol through the bloodstream.
entries.physiological_function: It delivers cholesterol to cells around the body, where cholesterol is used to build cell membranes and make certain hormones.

entries.name: HDL Cholesterol
entries.what_it_is: HDL (high-density lipoprotein) is a lipoprotein particle that carries cholesterol in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: It transports cholesterol away from tissues back towards the liver as part of normal cholesterol recycling.

entries.name: Total Cholesterol
entries.what_it_is: Cholesterol is a waxy lipid found in every cell of the body, measured here as a total across lipoprotein particles.
entries.physiological_function: The body uses cholesterol to build cell membranes, make vitamin D, and produce steroid hormones and bile acids.

entries.name: Triglycerides
entries.what_it_is: Triglycerides are the most common type of fat (lipid) in the body, carried in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: They store energy from food and release it between meals to fuel the body's cells.

entries.name: Glucose
entries.what_it_is: Glucose is a simple sugar carried in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: It is the body's main immediate energy source, taken up by cells and used to produce energy.

entries.name: HbA1c
entries.what_it_is: HbA1c is haemoglobin that has glucose attached to it, measured as a percentage or mmol/mol.
entries.physiological_function: Because red blood cells live for around three months, it reflects the average amount of glucose carried in the blood over that period.

entries.name: Insulin
entries.what_it_is: Insulin is a hormone made by the pancreas.
entries.physiological_function: It signals cells to take up glucose from the blood and helps the body store energy.

entries.name: Haemoglobin
entries.what_it_is: Haemoglobin is the iron-containing protein inside red blood cells.
entries.physiological_function: It binds oxygen in the lungs and carries it around the body to the tissues.

entries.name: Ferritin
entries.what_it_is: Ferritin is a protein that stores iron inside cells.
entries.physiological_function: It holds iron in a safe, stored form and releases it when the body needs to make new red blood cells.

entries.name: Iron
entries.what_it_is: Iron is an essential mineral measured here as the amount circulating in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: It is a key building block of haemoglobin and is used to carry oxygen and support energy production in cells.

entries.name: Transferrin
entries.what_it_is: Transferrin is a protein in the blood that binds iron.
entries.physiological_function: It transports iron through the bloodstream to where the body uses or stores it.

entries.name: Vitamin D
entries.what_it_is: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin made in the skin from sunlight and obtained from some foods.
entries.physiological_function: It helps the body absorb calcium and supports normal bone and muscle function.

entries.name: Vitamin B12
entries.what_it_is: Vitamin B12 is a water-soluble vitamin obtained from food.
entries.physiological_function: It is needed to make red blood cells, support nerve function, and help produce DNA.

entries.name: Folic acid
entries.what_it_is: Folate (folic acid) is a B vitamin obtained from food.
entries.physiological_function: It is used to make and repair DNA and to form new red blood cells.

entries.name: Calcium (adjusted)
entries.what_it_is: Calcium is an essential mineral, measured here in the blood and adjusted for protein levels.
entries.physiological_function: It builds bones and teeth and is involved in muscle movement, nerve signalling, and blood clotting.

entries.name: Magnesium
entries.what_it_is: Magnesium is an essential mineral found throughout the body.
entries.physiological_function: It supports hundreds of enzyme reactions, including those involved in energy production, muscle, and nerve function.

entries.name: Creatinine
entries.what_it_is: Creatinine is a waste product made by normal muscle activity.
entries.physiological_function: It is carried in the blood to the kidneys, which filter it out into the urine.

entries.name: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR)
entries.what_it_is: eGFR is a calculated estimate of how much blood the kidneys filter each minute.
entries.physiological_function: It reflects the kidneys' normal job of filtering waste products out of the blood.

entries.name: Alanine Aminotransferase (ALT)
entries.what_it_is: ALT is an enzyme found mainly in liver cells.
entries.physiological_function: It helps the liver process amino acids as part of normal metabolism.

entries.name: Aspartate Aminotransferase (AST)
entries.what_it_is: AST is an enzyme found in the liver, heart, and muscle cells.
entries.physiological_function: It takes part in normal amino-acid metabolism inside cells.

entries.name: Albumin
entries.what_it_is: Albumin is the most abundant protein in blood plasma, made by the liver.
entries.physiological_function: It helps keep fluid inside blood vessels and carries hormones, vitamins, and other substances around the body.

entries.name: Total Bilirubin
entries.what_it_is: Bilirubin is a yellow pigment produced when old red blood cells are broken down.
entries.physiological_function: It is processed by the liver and removed from the body in bile.

entries.name: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone (TSH)
entries.what_it_is: TSH is a hormone released by the pituitary gland in the brain.
entries.physiological_function: It signals the thyroid gland to produce thyroid hormones that regulate the body's metabolism.

entries.name: Free Thyroxine (FT4)
entries.what_it_is: FT4 (free thyroxine) is the unbound, active form of the main thyroid hormone in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: Thyroid hormone helps regulate the body's metabolic rate, temperature, and energy use.

entries.name: Testosterone
entries.what_it_is: Testosterone is a steroid sex hormone, present in higher amounts in men.
entries.physiological_function: It supports the development of male characteristics, muscle and bone, and reproductive function.

entries.name: High Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hsCRP)
entries.what_it_is: hsCRP is a protein made by the liver, measured with a sensitive test.
entries.physiological_function: It is part of the body's normal inflammatory response and rises and falls as part of that process.

entries.name: Platelet Count
entries.what_it_is: Platelets are small cell fragments in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: They clump together to help form clots and stop bleeding when a blood vessel is injured.

entries.name: White Blood Cell Count
entries.what_it_is: White blood cells are the cells of the immune system, counted here in the blood.
entries.physiological_function: They defend the body against infections and foreign substances.

entries.name: Uric Acid
entries.what_it_is: Uric acid is a waste product formed when the body breaks down substances called purines.
entries.physiological_function: It is carried in the blood and removed by the kidneys into the urine.

entries.name: Potassium
entries.what_it_is: Potassium is an essential mineral and electrolyte found mainly inside cells.
entries.physiological_function: It helps nerves and muscles work and supports a normal heartbeat and fluid balance.
